title: Underlying Rules of Evolutionary Urban Systems in Africa
markdown:
By [**Gang Xu**](https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=HQASxqUAAAAJ&hl=en) et al, 2025

**_Topic:_** Urbanisation; Urban Science; Science of Cities; Urban System; Urban Geography

**_Keywords:_** Urban growth; Zipf’s law; Gibrat’s law; Scaling law; Remote sensing; GIS

_**Africapolis data:**_ GIS, agglomeration data, country data

_**Link to the original article:**_ [Underlying Rules of Evolutionary Urban Systems in Africa](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44284-025-00208-y)
___


Urban systems, as they evolve, exhibit patterns that follow certain underlying rules, which can be represented through simple mathematical models such as Zipf’s Law (rank-size rule), Gibrat’s Law (proportionate growth), and scaling law. While much research has focused on the global applicability of these laws, less attention has been given to their regional variations at different stages of urbanisation, especially in Africa, a rapidly urbanising continent.

Africa, with its diverse geography, socio-economic variations, and multicultural influences, presents a unique case for studying urban evolution. These factors lead to distinctive urbanisation processes across the continent, providing key insights into the development of urban systems. Based on the Africapolis dataset, a recent study uncovered the evolution of African urban systems combining the three underlying rules, which has been published in *Nature Cities*.

### A Century of African Urbanisation

Over the past century, African urbanisation has seen profound changes, characterised by rapid growth in both the size and number of urban agglomerations. From 1950 to 2020, the number of urban agglomerations with populations exceeding 10,000 surged from 627 to 8,784 (Fig. 1a). The 21st century has seen particularly accelerated urban population growth in western and eastern Africa (Fig. 1b). By 2050, urbanisation levels in most African regions (except eastern Africa) are projected to exceed 60%, matching global averages (Fig. 1c). Africa’s share of the world’s newly added urban population has risen dramatically, from less than 5% in 1950 to 25% by 2020, underscoring the continent’s growing influence on global urbanisation trends (Fig. 1d).

##### *Figure 1. Evolution of African urban systems and urbanisation trends*
![img](/images/tempimages/GangXu_1.jpg)

### Towards a Centralised City-Size Distribution

This extensive urbanisation has not only increased urban populations but also transformed the hierarchy of African urban systems.

Rank-size plots of urban agglomerations show a consistent upward shift from 1950 to 2020, indicating a general increase in population sizes (Fig. 2a).

The Pareto exponent (α), reflecting the rank-size slope, has steadily approached 1, signalling the rise of larger cities and a shift from a dispersed to a more centralised city-size distribution (Fig. 2b-2d).

This trend is most pronounced as urbanisation intensifies (Fig. 2e-2f), confirming a shift towards Zipf’s law, which predicts that urban systems in Africa are aligning with global urbanisation patterns.

##### *Figure 2. Zipf’s law of the African urban system*
![img](/images/tempimages/GangXu_2.jpg)

### How Urban Growth Varies Across Different City Sizes

 [Gibrat’s law](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44284-025-00208-y#Sec8) holds that the distribution of city size follows a [lognormal distribution] (https://gregorygundersen.com/blog/2023/12/17/lognormal/), and urban population growth is independent of the initial city size. From 2010 to 2020, urban growth rates across Africa generally followed Gibrat’s law, showing scale-independent dynamics (Fig. 3a-3b). However, in earlier decades, such as the 1950s to 1960s and again in the 1980s, larger cities grew more rapidly than smaller ones (Fig. 3c). By the 1990s, growth patterns became more uniform, with cities of different sizes expanding proportionately (Fig. 3c). However, during the 1980s and 1990s, smaller cities in southern Africa grew faster (Fig. 3d). The increase in the Pareto exponent (α) by Zipf’s law mirrors this shift across southern Africa from 1990 to 2000 (Fig. 2c), contributing to a more dispersed city size distribution.

##### *Figure 3. Gibrat’s law of the African urban system*
![img](/images/tempimages/GangXu_3.jpg)

### Economies of Scale in Urban Land Use

In addition to population dynamics, the relationship between city size and built-up area was examined through urban scaling law. Generally, there is a sublinear relationship between built-up area and population size in most African countries, meaning that larger cities are more land-efficient (Fig. 4a). However, in eastern and western Africa, cities showed a nearly linear or even superlinear relationship between built-up area and population size, indicating more extensive urban development in larger cities (Fig. 4b). This is in contrast to urban systems in developed countries, where larger cities tend to be more land-efficient. However, as African urban systems mature, a gradual shift towards sublinear scaling is occurring, reflecting greater land-use efficiency in larger cities (Fig. 4c).

##### *Figure 4. Scaling law between built-up areas and population size in Africa*
![img](/images/tempimages/GangXu_4.jpg)

### Using Africapolis for African Urban Studies
The Africapolis database, which includes comprehensive GIS data on urban populations and land use, was pivotal for analysing the evolution of African urban systems. By applying the theories of Zipf’s law, Gibrat’s law, and scaling laws, this study not only verified the applicability of these universal urban laws but also highlighted their regional specificities. The results confirmed that African urban systems are evolving in a manner increasingly similar to global trends, with a concentration of population in larger cities and a shift towards more efficient urban land use as these systems mature.

### Author’s Biography

Gang Xu is an Assistant Professor in the Department of Geography at Wuhan University, China. His research interests are urban geography and complex urban systems.
